nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef environments and tide pools adjacent to reefs, typically found at depths of 0–10 m

diet.notes: Generally advised to offer a varied diet of meaty foods such as Mysis shrimp, krill, silversides, clams, and chopped crustacean items, fed two to three times daily. Algae supplements have been reported to be accepted. Puffers use their beak-like fused teeth to crush hard-shelled prey, and offering the occasional hard food (e.g. clams on the half shell, small snails) is thought to help wear down the teeth and prevent overgrowth.

goodWith: other semi-aggressive fish of similar size

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 5146x3431. Tack-sharp, vibrant Red Sea (Jeddah) individual showing brown body with pale blue spots and blue eye lines; whole fish in frame. Best pick.
Diego Delso, 4080x2720. Sharp side profile with fanned dorsal fin; different pose of a vibrant in-color specimen.
Jean-Paul Boerekamps, 1024x568. Darker in-situ individual near coral; correct ID with clear pale-blue spotting, adds source and color variety. Slightly softer, lower res.

summary: The Pearl Toby Puffer (Canthigaster margaritata) is a small Red Sea endemic reaching around 10–12 cm, recognizable by its brown body covered in pale blue spots. It tends to nip corals and consume ornamental invertebrates, so it is generally best suited to fish-only aquariums.
careNotes:
The Pearl Toby Puffer is endemic to the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, where it tends to inhabit shallow reef flats and tide pools. It is generally considered an intermediate-level species due to its dietary needs and semi-aggressive disposition.

This species tends to be best kept singly or, with caution, with other fish that are not small or easily bullied. It has been known to display aggression toward conspecifics and toward fish of similar coloration. Introduction into an established community should be done carefully.

Most sources consider this species not reef-safe: it has been documented nipping corals and will readily consume ornamental invertebrates including small crustaceans and molluscs. A fish-only-with-live-rock setup — sometimes called a FOWLR tank — is generally recommended, where live rock provides biological filtration and natural décor without the addition of coral or other invertebrates that the puffer may damage.

Puffers should not be transported with a net, as they may inflate when stressed. A container or cup should be used when moving or acclimating the fish.

This species' beak-like teeth are fused into a parrot-like structure; without appropriate hard-shelled prey items, the teeth may overgrow. Regular offerings of clams on the half shell or similar hard foods are commonly recommended.

Water quality should be maintained carefully, with good filtration and regular water changes, as puffers are generally considered heavy bioload producers.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Main husbandry source. Collection landing page used for initial discovery; product page fetched for detailed care parameters.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (11.5 cm TL), temperature range (24.7–29.2°C), native range (Red Sea and Gulf of Aden), and taxonomy. FishBase size is authoritative; the 31.2 cm figure from seatizens.sc was disregarded as a likely database error.

additional.name: Harry's Marine Life
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~4 inches), temperature (74–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), care level (moderate/intermediate), and semi-aggressive temperament. Tank size listed as 30 gallons, which was considered conservative.

additional.name: Seatizens
additional.notes: Confirmed facultative corallivore diet, shallow reef habitat, and IUCN Least Concern status. The 31.2 cm maximum length listed here was determined to be a data error and disregarded; typical specimens are cited as ~7 cm.
